title: Variables on Both Sides
learningTarget: I can solve equations where variables appear on both sides and decide whether the equation has one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions.
whyItMatters: Some equations compare two expressions that both have a variable. The key move is to collect the variable terms on one side, collect the constants on the other side, then read what the equation is telling you.
realWorldApplication: On CAASPP, variables-on-both-sides questions often show up as pricing plans, student work, or equations that ask whether there is one answer, no answer, or every answer. In real life, this helps you compare two plans and find when they become equal.

standards.label: Explain why each equation-solving step is valid.
Solve linear equations in one variable.
Create equations from real situations and use them to solve problems.

overviewPrompt:
Create a short overview video for 11th grade students preparing for the CAASPP math test.

Lesson title: Variables on Both Sides

Main idea: Some equations have variable terms on both sides. To solve them, move the variable terms to one side and the constant terms to the other side while keeping the equation balanced. Then divide to isolate x.

Use this exact example:

Solve 4x + 7 = 2x + 21.

Step 1: Move the smaller variable term.
Subtract 2x from both sides:
2x + 7 = 21

Step 2: Move the constant.
Subtract 7 from both sides:
2x = 14

Step 3: Divide.
Divide both sides by 2:
x = 7

Check:
4(7) + 7 = 2(7) + 21
28 + 7 = 14 + 21
35 = 35

The final answer is x = 7.

Also briefly explain the three possible outcomes:
One solution: x equals one specific number.
No solution: variables cancel and leave a false statement, like 10 = 15.
Infinitely many solutions: variables cancel and leave a true statement, like 10 = 10.

Keep it short, student-friendly, and focused on the idea of balance.

overviewVideo.sectionTitle: Start Here: Variables on Both Sides
overviewVideo.title: Lesson 3 Overview: Variables on Both Sides
overviewVideo.description: Watch this quick overview before you start solving. It shows how to move variable terms, keep both sides balanced, and recognize what kind of solution an equation has.

vocabulary.definition: A term with a variable, such as 4x or 2x.
A number without a variable, such as 7 or 21.
The equation is true for one specific value of the variable.
The variables cancel and leave a false statement, so no value works.
The variables cancel and leave a true statement, so every value works.

sections.title: Overview Reflection
sections.studentText: After watching the overview, explain the main point in your own words.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why moving variable terms to one side can make an equation easier to solve.
questions.feedback: A strong answer says that collecting variable terms on one side and constants on the other side makes it easier to isolate x while keeping the equation balanced.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.studentText: Before solving a full equation, decide which terms belong together.

questions.prompt: In 4x + 7 = 2x + 21, which terms are variable terms?
questions.feedback: Variable terms include the variable. Here, 4x and 2x are the variable terms.

sections.title: Watch: Solving Equations with Variables on Both Sides
sections.watchFor: Watch the exact equation 2x + 3 = 5x - 2. Notice how the teacher visualizes both sides, subtracts 2x, adds 2, divides by 3, and explains why 5/3 is the same as 1 2/3.

contextQuestions.prompt: In the video equation 2x + 3 = 5x - 2, why does the teacher subtract 2x from both sides first?

options.text: To remove the x terms from the left side and leave a positive number of x's on the right
To remove the constants from both sides
To make the equation have no solution

contextQuestions.feedback: Subtracting 2x from both sides removes the x terms from the left and leaves 3x on the right.

contextQuestions.prompt: After subtracting 2x from both sides, what equation is left?
contextQuestions.feedback: The 2x terms on the left cancel, and 5x - 2x leaves 3x, so the equation becomes 3 = 3x - 2.

contextQuestions.prompt: Why does the teacher add 2 to both sides after getting 3 = 3x - 2?
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says that adding 2 cancels the -2 on the right, keeps the equation balanced, and leaves 5 = 3x.

contextQuestions.prompt: When the video reaches 5 = 3x, why does the teacher divide both sides by 3?

options.text: Because x is being multiplied by 3, and division by 3 isolates x
Because every variables-on-both-sides equation ends by dividing by 3

contextQuestions.feedback: 3x means 3 times x. Dividing both sides by 3 leaves one x, so x = 5/3.

contextQuestions.prompt: The video says x = 5/3, which can also be written as 1 2/3. Explain why those two forms mean the same value.
contextQuestions.feedback: 5/3 is an improper fraction. Three thirds make 1 whole, with 2 thirds left over, so 5/3 = 1 2/3.

sections.title: Worked Example: Move Variables, Then Constants
sections.studentText: When x appears on both sides, collect the x terms first. Then move the constants. The goal is still the same: isolate the variable.

steps: Move variable terms: subtract 2x from both sides to get 2x + 7 = 21.
Move constants: subtract 7 from both sides to get 2x = 14.
Divide: divide both sides by 2 to get x = 7.
workedExample.commonMistake: A common mistake is subtracting 2x from one side only. Every legal move must happen to both sides.

sections.title: Hands-On: Choose the Next Balanced Move
sections.studentText: Use the undo board to solve 4x + 7 = 2x + 21. Choose the next legal move from the bank. The board only moves forward when your move keeps the equation balanced.

states.dropText: Choose the first move that gets variable terms together.
states.feedback: Start by moving the smaller variable term so the x terms are on one side.
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. Since x appears on both sides, first collect the variable terms.

states.dropText: Choose the move that clears the constant.
states.feedback: Good. Subtracting 2x from both sides leaves 2x + 7 = 21. Now clear the +7.
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. x is still not isolated because +7 is attached to 2x.

states.feedback: Good. Subtracting 7 leaves 2x = 14. What undoes multiplying by 2?
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. x is being multiplied by 2, so use the inverse operation.

states.feedback: Solved: x = 7. You moved variables, moved constants, then divided.

sections.title: Real-World Equation: Photo Booth Packages
sections.studentTextHtml:
Scenario: A school event is comparing two photo booth packages.

Classic Booth: $18 setup fee plus $4 per photo strip.

Deluxe Booth: $6 setup fee plus $7 per photo strip.

Question: After how many photo strips will the two packages cost the same?

Equation: 4x + 18 = 7x + 6

image.alt: Students comparing two photo booth package tables at a school event.
image.caption: Two photo booth packages can start with different fees and grow at different rates. Your job is to find when the costs match.

questions.prompt: In the equation 4x + 18 = 7x + 6, what does x represent?
questions.feedback: x represents the number of photo strips.
Subtract 4x from both sides: 18 = 3x + 6. Subtract 6: 12 = 3x. Divide by 3: x = 4.

questions.prompt: Explain what x = 4 means in the photo booth situation.
questions.feedback: x = 4 means the two packages cost the same after 4 photo strips. Both plans cost $34.
Subtract 2x to get 4x + 5 = 21. Subtract 5 to get 4x = 16. x = 4.

questions.prompt: Solve: 3x + 10 = 3x + 4. What kind of solution does it have?
questions.feedback: Subtract 3x from both sides and you get 10 = 4, which is false. No solution.

questions.prompt: Solve: 5x + 8 = 5x + 8. What kind of solution does it have?
questions.feedback: The two sides are identical. Every value of x keeps the equation true.

questions.prompt: Explain how you know whether an equation has no solution after the variables cancel.
questions.feedback: If the variables cancel and the remaining statement is false, like 10 = 4, then no value works.
Subtract 2x to get 3x + 3 = 18. Subtract 3 to get 3x = 15. x = 5.

questions.prompt: What is the first strong move for 8x - 6 = 3x + 14?
questions.feedback: Subtracting 3x moves the variable terms together and keeps the equation balanced.

questions.prompt: Solve: 4x + 9 = 4x - 2. What kind of solution does it have?
questions.feedback: Subtract 4x and you get 9 = -2, which is false. No solution.

questions.prompt: Solve: 2(3x + 5) = 6x + 10. What kind of solution does it have?
questions.feedback: Distribute to get 6x + 10 = 6x + 10. This is always true, so there are infinitely many solutions.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain how variables on both sides can lead to one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions.
commonMisconceptions: variables cancel always means no solution
questions.feedback: A strong answer says that if solving gives x equal to one number, there is one solution; if variables cancel and leave a false statement, there is no solution; if variables cancel and leave a true statement, there are infinitely many solutions.

gate.completion: Student attempts the overview reflection, video questions, real-world task, practice, and exit check.
gate.mastery: 70% or higher across the lesson, with the exit check flagged for teacher follow-up if it falls below 70%.
gate.remediation: Practice collecting variable terms on one side, then classify true and false statements after variables cancel.
gate.enrichment: Create two pricing plans that become equal at a chosen number of items, then prove the solution.
